findings.title: Sandbox executor debug log exposes full args including env var values
findings.detail: Line 20: `tracing::debug!("Sandbox executing: {} {:?}", plan.program, plan.args)`. For install-class commands that use /usr/bin/env, the plan.args vector contains the full `KEY=VALUE` env entries passed to the sandbox (built in macos.rs lines 66-68 and linux.rs lines 53-57). If a user has set e.g. CARGO_HOME or HOME to a path that embeds a secret, or if future callers populate plan.args with credentials, those values will appear in debug-level trace logs. The `filtered_env()` allow-list restricts what goes into env (PATH, HOME, USER, SHELL, LANG, LC_ALL, LC_CTYPE, TERM, CARGO_HOME, RUSTUP_HOME), so the risk is low today, but the pattern is structurally unsafe: any future code that adds sensitive env vars to SandboxRequest.env will silently log them at debug level.
findings.recommendation: Replace the debug log with a redacted form that prints env variable names but not values: log `plan.args` with values masked for env-style tokens (`KEY=...` format detection). At minimum, add a comment warning that env values appear in the debug output.

findings.title: OAuth state parameter not verified against PKCE state before code exchange
findings.detail: The callback server extracts both `code` and `state` from the redirect (lines 65-105) and returns them to the caller as `OAuthCallback { code, state }`. However, `callback.rs` itself does not compare `state` against the PKCE `state` that was generated before the auth redirect. The responsibility is delegated to the caller (crates/ava-tui/src/auth.rs). If any call site omits or incorrectly implements this comparison, CSRF attacks on the OAuth flow become possible. A malicious page that initiates a concurrent auth flow could substitute its own authorization code.
findings.recommendation: Accept the expected `state` value as a parameter to `listen_for_callback()` and reject the callback with `AuthError` if state does not match. This makes CSRF protection structural and eliminates the risk of call-site omission.

findings.title: rm-classifier bypass via `find -exec rm -rf`
findings.detail: The `check_blocked_patterns` function only inspects commands whose first token (lowercased) is `rm`. A command such as `find / -exec rm -rf {} +` or `find /etc -delete` would bypass the critical-path rm block entirely: `find` is not in the block list, and neither is the `-delete` flag or `exec rm`. The whole-command lowercase check and the per-part POSIX tokenizer would both classify this as Low risk. This represents a semantic bypass of the rm protection that has already had four rounds of hardening.
findings.recommendation: Add `find` with `-exec rm` and `-delete` to `check_blocked_patterns`. At minimum, scan all command parts for any `rm -rf`-equivalent sub-invocations even when the leading command word is not `rm`.

findings.title: SBPL escape does not reject newlines or NUL bytes
findings.detail: The `escape_sbpl_path` function rejects `"`, `\`, `(`, and `)`. It does not reject `\n` (newline), `\r`, or `\0` (NUL). SBPL is whitespace-delimited; a path containing a newline could potentially inject a new SBPL rule on a separate line. Paths containing NUL bytes can cause OS-level issues with C string handling in sandbox-exec. In practice macOS rejects NUL-containing paths at the filesystem level, so severity is low, but the function's intent is to be a strict allowlist.
findings.recommendation: Add a check for any character with ASCII value < 0x20 (control characters including NUL, LF, CR, TAB) in addition to the existing rejections.

findings.title: Secret redaction does not cover Anthropic or OpenRouter key formats
findings.detail: The `PATTERNS` block covers: sk-proj-*, sk-*, AKIA*, AWS secret assignments, Bearer tokens, gh[ps]_*, github_pat_*, and generic `secret|password|token|api_key|auth` assignments. Anthropic API keys use the format `sk-ant-api03-...`, which starts with `sk-` and would be caught by the `sk_key` pattern. However, OpenRouter keys use the format `sk-or-v1-...`, which is also caught. This appears safe. The gap is Gemini API keys (format `AIza[A-Za-z0-9-_]{35}`) and Inception keys, which share no common prefix with covered patterns and would only be caught by the generic assignment pattern if the variable name contains `api_key`/`token`/etc. A bare Gemini key printed in command output would not be redacted.
findings.recommendation: Add a pattern for `AIza[A-Za-z0-9\-_]{35}` to cover Gemini API keys.

findings.title: Session JSONL logger does not redact message content before writing to disk
findings.detail: The `TurnLogEntry` written to `~/.ava/log/{session_id}.jsonl` includes `tool_calls: Vec<TurnToolCall>` which only stores tool name and call ID, not arguments. Content is not logged. However, the future evolution risk is real: if `TurnLogEntry` is extended to include tool arguments or response content, those will be written verbatim to disk. No redaction step is applied at the logger boundary. The `redact_secrets()` function exists in `ava-tools` but is not imported here.
findings.recommendation: Document in a code comment that `TurnLogEntry` must never include raw tool arguments or LLM response content. If content logging is added in future, pipe it through `redact_secrets()` before writing.

findings.title: Sub-agent spawner inherits extended_tools=true without permission middleware
findings.detail: At line 687, `AgentTaskSpawner::spawn_named()` sets `extended_tools: true` in the sub-agent `AgentConfig`. The sub-agent's `ToolRegistry` is built with `register_core_tools()` only (line 655-656), without attaching the permission middleware that the parent agent has (approval bridge, permission inspector, etc.). This means sub-agents run with extended tool access but without the parent's safety gates: no approval prompts, no command classification, no `filtered_env()` call for bash commands executed by a sub-agent. The sub-agent's bash tool will use the process's full environment (not scrubbed) unless the platform's LocalShell happens to be called with scrub_env=true, which depends on the BashTool implementation within the sub-agent's tool set.
findings.recommendation: Pass the parent's permission inspector and approval bridge to sub-agent tool registries, or explicitly document and test that sub-agents are constrained to a safe subset of tools. Verify that the BashTool used by sub-agents calls execute_with_options with scrub_env=true.

sbpl_injection.detail: escape_sbpl_path() at macos.rs:9-16 rejects paths containing '"', '\\', '(', ')'. Test at line 96 covers the injection pattern. The fix is correct. One minor gap exists (control chars, see R5-S1) but it does not affect known SBPL injection vectors.

env_scrubbing.detail: Both execution paths in bash.rs call filtered_env() and pass scrub_env:true. The sandbox path (lines 87-88) uses filtered_env() in the SandboxRequest.env field, and the non-sandbox path (lines 122-124) passes filtered_env() and scrub_env:true to execute_with_options. The LocalShell implementation honors scrub_env correctly at shell.rs:84-87 and 159-162. No missing path found.

rm_rf_blocklist.detail: The POSIX tokenizer + collect_flags() correctly handles -rf, -fr, -r -f, -f -r, quoted paths, and double-slash normalization. 17 critical path prefixes are protected. Wildcard top-level globs (/etc/*) are blocked. Legitimate paths under prefixes (/home/user/project) are correctly allowed. Gap: find -exec rm and find -delete are not covered (see R5-W3).

credential_handling_assessment.Debug_format_for_ProviderCredential: SAFE — custom Debug impl at credentials.rs:41-78 redacts api_key, oauth_token, oauth_refresh_token.
credential_handling_assessment.API_keys_in_provider_structs: SAFE — api_key fields are used only in HTTP header construction; no tracing calls log them.
credential_handling_assessment.OAuth_PKCE_state: AT_RISK — state comparison is delegated to call sites; not enforced at callback boundary (see R5-W2).
credential_handling_assessment.Credential_file_permissions: NOT_AUDITED — write_file_atomic at credentials.rs:170 saves to ~/.ava/credentials.json but file mode (0600 vs 0644) not visible in this audit scope.
credential_handling_assessment.Env_var_API_keys: SAFE — env_override_credential() reads standard env vars but never logs them; the placeholder check prevents dummy values from leaking as credentials.
